chunk_summary: Indian human milk banks require formal quality assurance systems, including SOPs, operational metrics, and corrective actions to ensure safety and traceability in resource-limited settings.
learning_objectives: Understand the components of quality assurance systems in Indian human milk banks.
Learn key operational metrics for monitoring milk bank safety and efficiency.
Identify corrective actions for contamination or equipment failure in milk banking.
Explore low-cost safety controls suitable for resource-limited settings.
key_facts: Indian guidelines mandate written SOPs for donor recruitment, screening, pasteurization, and dispensing.
Operational metrics include time from donation to pasteurization and discard rates due to cold-chain failure.
Core documentation includes temperature logs, culture results, and donor screening records.
Periodic drills trace dispensed milk back to donor screening and pasteurization records.
Low-cost controls like daily temperature charting and sealed storage bins improve safety in resource-limited settings.
Corrective actions for contamination include stock quarantine, clinical notification, and staff retraining.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Indian national guidance for lactation management centers emphasizes written SOPs for donor recruitment, screening, collection, pooling, pasteurization, culture testing, storage, and dispensing.
document_key_topics: Quality assurance systems in human milk banks
Operational metrics for milk bank safety
Corrective actions for contamination or equipment failure
Low-cost safety controls in resource-limited settings
